note: Pre-filled interview answers for Mario Lizarraga based on his real work history at Safran, Jenton, Honeywell, and El Garage. Use as starting points — personalize and practice out loud.

meta.0:
At Safran, our UFactory 850 robot arm test system started failing intermittently during production — random force readings spiking, causing false test failures on about 15% of panels. Production was backing up.

I took ownership of debugging it. First, I pulled the force data logs from our monitoring platform and plotted the failure patterns in Python (SciPy). I noticed the spikes correlated with specific fixture positions, not random panels. That pointed to a mechanical issue, not electrical.

I inspected the fixture and found the 3D-printed end-effector had developed micro-cracks after thousands of press cycles — the alignment was shifting by ~0.3mm under load. I redesigned the fixture in SolidWorks with reinforced geometry and switched from FDM to SLA printing for better layer adhesion.

After installing the new fixture, false failures dropped to under 1%. I documented the failure mode and added it to our preventive maintenance schedule — now we inspect fixtures every 2,000 cycles.
meta.1:
When building the robot arm test system at Safran, I had to decide between building a perfect, fully-automated system or shipping a working MVP that still required some manual steps.

The legacy test equipment was failing weekly, costing 3+ days of downtime per month. I could spend 6 months building the ideal system, or 6 weeks shipping something that worked for 80% of panels.

I shipped the 80% version first. It handled the most common panel types automatically, and technicians manually loaded the remaining 20%. I then iterated — adding fixture adapters, improving the software, automating more panel types over the next few months.

The result: we went from 30 min/panel to 10 min/panel within 6 weeks instead of waiting 6 months. The remaining 20% got automated over the following quarter.
meta.2:
I'd start with requirements gathering: what are we testing? What are the pass/fail criteria? What's the expected volume? Then:

1. Define test specifications — electrical, mechanical, environmental parameters
2. Design the test fixture — how the DUT interfaces with sensors and actuators
3. Select instrumentation — force sensors, position sensors, cameras, whatever the test needs
4. Design the data acquisition pipeline — sampling rates, filtering, data storage
5. Write the control software — sequence of operations, safety interlocks
6. Build and validate — first with known-good and known-bad samples
7. Write test procedures and train operators
8. Deploy with monitoring — track test yield, false failure rates, equipment health

At Safran, I did exactly this for our switch panel test system: UFactory 850 arm + FUTEK load cell + STM32 continuity tester + Python data pipeline. The key was iterating the fixture design — it took 4+ revisions to get sub-millimeter alignment.
meta.3:
At Safran, I proposed using 3D-printed fixtures for the robot arm test system. A colleague in manufacturing argued we should machine them from aluminum for durability.

Instead of pushing my preference, I set up a structured comparison: I printed a fixture and machined one, then ran both through 1,000 test cycles while logging dimensional accuracy. The data showed the SLA-printed fixture held tolerance for ~2,000 cycles before degradation, while aluminum was effectively permanent but cost 10x more and had a 3-week lead time.

I presented the data and proposed a hybrid approach: SLA for development and iteration (fast, cheap), with a plan to machine the final production version once the design stabilized. My colleague agreed because the data made the trade-off clear. We ended up going through 4+ design revisions — if we'd machined each one, it would have taken months instead of days.
meta.4:
I work with FUTEK load cells for force measurement — capturing force-vs-displacement curves during robotic press cycles. The system samples at high frequency, and I built a Python pipeline using SciPy for signal processing: Savitzky-Golay smoothing, peak detection, travel measurement, and inflection point identification.

I also integrated thermocouples (MAX31850K via 1-Wire on Raspberry Pi 5) for our paint cure monitoring system — sampling oven temperatures every 5 seconds with WiFi offline buffering to MariaDB.

For vibration, I installed accelerometers on test fixtures to establish dynamic load profiles and validate design improvements. The key challenge was noise — I used median filters and multi-scale smoothing to get clean signals from noisy industrial environments.
meta.5:
The biggest impact was building the robot arm test system from scratch. Before I arrived, each illuminated cockpit panel was tested manually — a technician would press every switch by hand, check force and continuity, and record results. It took 30 minutes per panel.

I designed and built an automated system: UFactory 850 robot arm presses each switch, FUTEK load cell measures force curves, STM32 board checks electrical continuity. Total test time: 10 minutes per panel.

With 75 panels per week, that's 25+ hours of manual labor freed every week — 1,300+ hours per year. I measure it directly: test time per panel (logged by the system) and monthly labor hours allocated to testing (tracked in our monitoring platform).
meta.6:
Step 1: Define requirements — what does 'qualified' mean? Performance specs, safety specs, reliability targets (MTBF), environmental conditions.

Step 2: Risk analysis — what can go wrong? Mechanical failures, electrical faults, software bugs, operator errors. Each risk gets a mitigation plan.

Step 3: Write the test plan — functional tests (does it work?), stress tests (does it work under load?), endurance tests (does it work after 10,000 cycles?), safety tests (does it fail safely?).

Step 4: Build and execute — run tests with known-good and known-bad samples. Document everything.

Step 5: Acceptance criteria review — did it meet all specs? Any conditional passes? Any waivers needed?

Step 6: Deployment — install, train operators, create maintenance schedule, set up monitoring.

At Safran, I took our robot arm system through this entire process. The endurance testing was critical — that's where we found the fixture degradation issue and redesigned for longer life.
meta.7:
When I needed to build the STM32 continuity tester for our robot arm system, I had never written C firmware for STM32 before. My background was Python and C# (Unity).

I gave myself a week. I started with STM32CubeIDE tutorials, then looked at example projects for serial communication and GPIO. Within 3 days I had a working prototype reading pin states and sending results over UART. By the end of the week, I'd added debounce logic, multi-channel scanning, and error handling.

The firmware is now in production — it validates electrical continuity on every switch during robotic press cycles. What made it work was focusing on the specific problem (serial comms + GPIO reads), not trying to learn all of embedded programming at once.
meta.9:
At Safran, our robot arm test system started having intermittent failures — panels that should pass were failing randomly. The technicians assumed it was a robot calibration issue and wanted me to recalibrate.

Instead of recalibrating blindly, I pulled 2 weeks of force data from our monitoring platform and analyzed it in Python. I plotted every test cycle's force curve and colored them by pass/fail. A clear pattern emerged: failures correlated with higher ambient temperature in the afternoon — the fixture was thermally expanding, shifting alignment.

The root cause wasn't calibration — it was thermal expansion of the 3D-printed fixture. I redesigned with a material that had lower thermal expansion coefficient and added temperature compensation to the acceptance criteria.

False failure rate dropped from ~15% to under 1%. Without diving into the data, we would have been recalibrating weekly and never fixing the real problem.
meta.12:
I supervised 5 interns across 2 years at Safran, working on AI defect detection and robotics projects.

My approach: I structured each intern's project with clear milestones (2-week sprints), gave them ownership of a defined scope, and met with them 3x/week for guidance. I didn't hand them solutions — I taught them to break problems down and find answers.

The result: they delivered a 92% accuracy ML classification model for visual defect detection on illuminated panels, plus a hardware prototype for camera-based inspection. One intern's work became the foundation for our vision inspection gantry project.

The key was matching project scope to skill level — I gave the stronger programmers the ML model work and the mechanically-oriented intern the camera mounting and fixture design.

microsoft.0:
When I was building the monitoring platform at Safran, I initially designed it as a simple dashboard — just displaying data. I showed it to the operations team and they said 'this is nice but we don't need another screen to watch.'

I had failed to understand the real user need. They didn't want a dashboard — they wanted automated alerts so they could focus on production instead of watching screens.

I redesigned the platform with automated notifications: equipment health alerts, downtime tracking, failure mode trending, and proactive maintenance reminders. That version reduced unplanned downtime from 3 days/month to 30 minutes every 6 months.

What I learned: always start with user interviews, not assumptions. I now validate every tool design with the actual end users before building. The prettiest dashboard is useless if nobody looks at it.
microsoft.1:
I would structure the testing around these layers:

1. Protocol validation — verify communication between controllers (serial, I2C, Modbus). Use packet sniffers and loopback tests.
2. Functional testing — verify each control loop works: sensors read correctly, actuators respond correctly, safety interlocks trigger.
3. Stress testing — push to extremes: max load, rapid cycling, network latency injection, power fluctuation simulation.
4. Failure mode testing — deliberately fail components: what happens when a sensor dies? When communication drops? Does the system fail safely?
5. Environmental testing — if applicable: temperature cycling, vibration, humidity.
6. Integration testing — full system with all subsystems running simultaneously.

At Safran I did similar work: testing the robot arm control system for reliability meant validating the FUTEK sensor readings, STM32 comms, and robot arm movements all together under production conditions.
microsoft.5:
When I joined Safran, everything was manual — testing, tracking, reporting. The company culture was 'we've always done it this way.'

Rather than pushing for wholesale change, I started small. I built the robot arm test system as a proof of concept alongside the existing manual process. Once technicians saw it cut their testing time from 30 to 10 minutes, they became advocates.

Then I extended the same approach to monitoring: I built the platform, showed the operations team how much downtime data they were missing, and let the results speak. Within months, the whole team was asking 'can you automate this too?'

What I learned: change management isn't about convincing people with arguments — it's about showing results at a small scale and letting success create demand.
microsoft.7:
At Safran, I built a monitoring platform using Python (FastAPI) with a SQL database backend, REST APIs for data ingestion, and a web frontend for dashboards.

The architecture: sensors and equipment send data via API calls to the FastAPI backend. The backend validates, stores in SQL, and runs scheduled analysis (failure trending, downtime calculations). Automated notifications trigger on thresholds (temperature out of range, downtime exceeding target, maintenance due).

I chose this architecture over Grafana because we needed custom business logic: work order tracking, oven cure time validation, and cross-referencing equipment failures with production batches. Off-the-shelf tools couldn't do that.

The system reduced unplanned downtime from 3 days/month to 30 minutes every 6 months — primarily because we could now see failure trends and act before they caused outages.
microsoft.11:
To me, growth mindset means treating every challenge as a learning opportunity, not a judgment of my abilities. It's the difference between 'I can't do this' and 'I can't do this yet.'

Concrete example: when I needed STM32 firmware for the continuity tester, I'd never written embedded C. Instead of defaulting to 'that's not my skillset, we should hire a firmware engineer,' I spent a week learning — tutorials, example code, trial and error. By the end, I had working production firmware.

I also apply growth mindset to feedback. When the operations team told me my monitoring dashboard wasn't useful, I didn't get defensive — I asked 'what would make it useful?' and rebuilt it with automated alerts based on their input.

Growth mindset isn't just about learning new skills — it's about being genuinely open to being wrong.

amazon.0:
At Safran, my job description was 'Automation Engineer' — focused on the robot arm test system. But I noticed that equipment downtime tracking was completely manual: technicians wrote issues in a notebook, and nobody analyzed patterns.

I took ownership beyond my scope. I designed and built a full monitoring platform from scratch — Python backend, SQL database, REST APIs, web dashboard. It tracks work orders, oven temperatures, robot downtime, failure modes, and sends automated alerts.

The result: unplanned downtime dropped from 3 days/month to 30 minutes every 6 months. Not because the equipment got better — but because we could now see problems forming and act before they caused outages.

I did this without being asked. I saw a gap, quantified the impact (downtime cost), built a solution, and delivered it. That's ownership.
amazon.1:
Manual switch testing at Safran: a technician presses every switch on an illuminated cockpit panel, checks force and continuity by hand, and records results manually. 30 minutes per panel, error-prone, boring work.

I simplified it to: robot presses switch, sensor measures force, controller checks continuity, software logs result. 10 minutes per panel, consistent, no human error.

The key simplification insight was that 80% of the complexity was in panel fixturing — each panel has different switch layouts. Instead of building a custom fixture for each panel type, I designed modular adapters that snap onto a universal base plate. That one decision reduced fixture inventory from dozens of custom jigs to one base + small adapters.

67% time reduction, 1,300+ hours freed per year, and the system handles 75 panels/week without breaks.
amazon.2:
When I was designing the robot arm test system, I had to choose between the UFactory 850 and a more expensive Fanuc cobot. I had specs from both vendors but no hands-on experience with either.

The UFactory was $15K cheaper but had less community support. The Fanuc had better documentation but was overkill for our force requirements. I had 3 days to decide before the budget cycle closed.

I chose UFactory based on: (1) our force requirements were well within its specs, (2) the Python SDK meant faster development, (3) the $15K savings funded the FUTEK sensors and STM32 hardware. I accepted the risk of less support because our application was straightforward — pick-and-press, not complex path planning.

The decision paid off. The system has been running in production for 2+ years. I documented every integration challenge to de-risk future purchases.
amazon.3:
When our robot arm test system produced unexpected force data, I didn't accept the surface explanation ('recalibrate the robot'). I pulled 2 weeks of data — every press cycle — into Python.

I built analysis scripts in SciPy: Savitzky-Golay smoothing for noise reduction, peak detection for max force identification, and statistical analysis across all test cycles. I plotted force curves colored by pass/fail, by time of day, by panel type, by fixture position.

The pattern was clear: failures spiked in the afternoon. Cross-referencing with temperature logs showed thermal expansion of the 3D-printed fixture was shifting alignment by 0.3mm — enough to cause false failures.

Root cause: material choice, not calibration. Fix: switched to a low-thermal-expansion material. False failures dropped from 15% to under 1%. Without diving deep into the data, we would have wasted months recalibrating.
amazon.4:
When I started at Safran, the robot arm test system didn't exist. Test procedures were informal — each technician had their own approach. Results were inconsistent and undocumented.

I created the standard: formal test plans with pass/fail criteria, documented test procedures, calibration schedules, and a monitoring system that tracks every result. I created 7+ service documents covering setup, calibration, troubleshooting, and failure modes.

This wasn't just about my system — I set the expectation that all test equipment should have this level of documentation. When I onboarded 5 interns, the documents enabled them to operate and maintain the system independently within a week.

Raising the bar meant making quality the default, not the exception. Now any engineer can pick up where I left off because the standards are documented, not just in my head.
amazon.8:
The full architecture: Problem was manual switch testing — 30 min/panel, technician shortage, inconsistent results, legacy equipment failing.

Component selection: UFactory 850 (6-DOF, 5kg payload, Python SDK) for the arm. FUTEK load cell for force measurement — needed sub-Newton resolution. STM32 microcontroller for continuity testing — custom PCB with multiplexed GPIO.

Make vs buy: I bought the robot and sensors (commodity items where vendor expertise matters). I built the fixtures (custom to our panel geometry), firmware (no off-the-shelf solution for our continuity test), and software (needed integration with our monitoring platform).

Prototyping: 4+ fixture revisions in SolidWorks, FDM then SLA printing. Each revision improved alignment precision. Final design achieves sub-millimeter repeatability.

Validation: tested with known-good and known-bad panels. Compared results to manual testing for 100 panels — matched 99%+ agreement.

Deployment: trained 3 technicians, wrote 7+ service documents, connected to monitoring platform for production tracking.
amazon.9:
When selecting the FUTEK load cell for our robot arm system, I evaluated 3 sensor vendors. FUTEK claimed sub-Newton accuracy and millisecond response time.

I didn't take the spec sheet at face value. I ordered evaluation units from FUTEK and a competitor, then designed a comparison test: apply known weights (calibrated masses), measure at various rates, check linearity, repeatability, and drift over temperature.

FUTEK's sensor matched their claims — the competitor showed 3x more drift at elevated temperatures. That data justified the 40% price premium. I also negotiated volume pricing and established a spare parts agreement.

For the UFactory robot arm, I ran a similar validation: tested repeatability at our press forces, verified the Python SDK worked for our control flow, and stress-tested for 10,000 cycles before committing to production deployment.
